serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Penang Char Kway Teow weighs approximately 350 g, as typically served at a hawker stall or zi char restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Char Kway Teow (Fried Flat Rice Noodles with Prawns)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) Foodo / My Healthy Plate Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Char Kway Teow as a standard hawker dish. Penang-style Char Kway Teow is closely analogous in composition; values are adapted from the HPB Char Kway Teow entry (per 100 g cooked). Penang-style typically uses lard and cockles, which may slightly elevate fat and cholesterol; values reflect the HPB standard entry as the best available approved-source anchor. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not publish a specific Penang Char Kway Teow entry. FAO/INFOODS SEA data for stir-fried rice noodle dishes corroborates the macro range.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and stir-fried noodle dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Penang Char Kway Teow. The University of Sydney GI Database reports a GI of approximately 40–55 for cooked flat rice noodles (kway teow / rice stick noodles) in mixed stir-fried dishes. A GI of 50 is used as the central estimate for this dish, consistent with the GI of rice noodle-based stir-fries reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) and the Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 72.8) / 100 = 36.4, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.8 × 3.5 = 72.8 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g; ~1680 mg per standard 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Penang Char Kway Teow due to its very high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning, which can significantly contribute to daily sodium intake exceeding the recommended 2000 mg limit in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~50) but high glycemic load per serving (~36.4) due to large portion of refined rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Penang Char Kway Teow in small portions, as the high carbohydrate load from refined flat rice noodles produces a substantial glycemic load per serving despite a moderate GI, and should pair it with additional protein or fibre to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~2.4 g/100 g), cholesterol (~85 mg/100 g), and high sodium from lard, cockles, and soy sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Penang Char Kway Teow due to its combination of saturated fat from lard and Chinese sausage, dietary cholesterol from cockles and eggs, and high sodium, all of which are established risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Penang Char Kway Teow (槟城炒粿条) is a iconic Malaysian stir-fried flat rice noodle dish originating from Penang, Malaysia, and widely regarded as one of the most celebrated hawker dishes in Southeast Asia. The dish is prepared by wok-frying broad, flat rice noodles (kway teow) over very high heat with prawns, cockles, Chinese sausage (lap cheong), bean sprouts, eggs, garlic, chives, dark soy sauce, light soy sauce, and chilli paste. Authentic Penang-style preparation traditionally uses lard rendered from pork fat, which imparts a distinctive smoky, rich flavour known as 'wok hei'. The noodles are soft and slightly chewy, with a savoury, umami-rich, mildly sweet and spicy flavour profile. Penang Char Kway Teow differs from the Singaporean version in its use of cockles, lard, and a darker, more intensely flavoured sauce. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 163 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (6.2 g), moderate fat (6.5 g, including 2.4 g saturated fat), and a significant carbohydrate content (20.8 g) from the rice noodles. Sodium is high at approximately 480 mg per 100 g, making a standard 350 g serving contribute approximately 1680 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 50 (low-moderate), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~36.4) due to the large noodle portion. Clinically, this dish is of concern for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or Type 2 Diabetes, and should be consumed in moderation. It is not suitable for halal diets due to lard and pork sausage, and contains multiple allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, eggs, and soy.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo Char Kway Teow entry, which is the closest approved-source match. No exact Penang Char Kway Teow entry exists in HPB Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA with full nutrient profiles. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice noodle-based stir-fried dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The input food_name 'Penang Char Kway Chao' appears to be a romanization variant or typographic error; the canonical name is 'Penang Char Kway Teow' (also spelled 'Char Koay Teow'). The alias 'Penang Char Kway Chao' is preserved in aliases. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo Char Kway Teow (per 100 g cooked); Penang-style preparation with lard may slightly elevate fat and cholesterol relative to the HPB entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this dish. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. potassium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. calcium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. iron_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. 4. The dish is not halal due to the traditional use of lard and pork-derived Chinese sausage. 5. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for Penang Char Kway Teow is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input listed cuisine_tags including 'Crab', which does not apply to standard Char Kway Teow; this tag has been disregarded as it likely reflects a restaurant menu tagging error. 7. Price data (SGD 18.00) from field survey data is preserved as contextual metadata only and is not used for nutritional derivation.
